food_subgroup: Freshwater fish curry with spice paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a rohu fish entry (FOODON_03302757) but no specific entry for Ruhi Sarso Bata (rohu with mustard paste preparation). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers rohu fish as a raw ingredient. No specific code exists for the mustard-paste preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Ruhi Sarso Bata or rohu fish dish. SNOMED CT does not cover this prepared dish.

preparation_method: spiced with mustard seeds and green chilli

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Ruhi Sarso Bata is approximately 250 g, comprising one or two pieces of rohu fish coated and simmered in a pungent mustard paste sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Rohu fish (raw) and mustard-based fish curry composition
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides rohu fish (raw) and fish curry with mustard data. Values for Ruhi Sarso Bata are adapted from ICMR-NIN rohu fish cooked values and mustard-paste fish curry entries. Mustard oil and mustard paste contribution to fat and energy is incorporated. Per-100g values reflect the cooked dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — fish and protein-dominant dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ruhi Sarso Bata. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous protein-dominant fish dishes with negligible carbohydrate content from the University of Sydney GI Database. Fish-based dishes with minimal starch consistently fall in the GI 25–35 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 5.25) / 100 = 1.6, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 5.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt and mustard paste preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving provides approximately 800 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, Ruhi Sarso Bata has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from rohu fish and mustard oil; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rohu fish provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and mustard oil contributes alpha-linolenic acid, both cardioprotective; saturated fat is low at 1.2 g per 100 g, making this dish appropriate for a heart-healthy diet when sodium is controlled.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ruhi Sarso Bata is a traditional Bengali fish dish made from rohu (Labeo rohita), a large freshwater carp widely consumed across West Bengal, Bangladesh, and the Indian subcontinent. The dish is prepared by marinating rohu fish pieces in turmeric and salt, then coating them in a freshly ground paste (bata) of black and yellow mustard seeds blended with green chilli and water. The fish is typically shallow-fried in mustard oil before being simmered in the mustard paste sauce, which gives the dish its characteristic sharp, pungent, and slightly bitter flavour with a warming heat from the chilli. The texture of the fish is firm and flaky, while the sauce is thick and intensely aromatic. Sarso Bata (mustard paste) is the defining element of this preparation, distinguishing it from other Bengali fish curries. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 16 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (8.5 g per 100 g) with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio due to mustard oil, and very low in carbohydrates (2.1 g per 100 g). It provides omega-3 fatty acids, B vitamins including B12, phosphorus, and iron. The glycemic index is low (estimated 30), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore, this dish is served at Bengali and North-East Indian restaurants and represents an authentic regional preparation from the Bengal culinary tradition.
common_user_queries: What fish is used in Sarso Bata?
Is Shorshe Rui the same as Ruhi Sarso Bata?
Is Ruhi Sarso Bata good for diabetics?
Is rohu fish high in omega-3?
Is Ruhi Sarso Bata gluten free?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN provides rohu fish and mustard-based fish curry data for the Indian subcontinent, which is the highest-priority approved source for this Indian dish. Values are adapted from rohu cooked entries and mustard fish curry composition rather than an exact match for Ruhi Sarso Bata as a named dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant fish dishes. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this fish preparation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for rohu fish or mustard fish curry. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in mustard oil and freshwater fish preparations per ICMR-NIN data. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and other micronutrients are estimated from ICMR-NIN rohu fish cooked values and mustard oil fatty acid profile. The dish is also known as Shorshe Rui in Bengali, where 'shorshe' means mustard and 'rui' is the Bengali name for rohu fish. The term 'sarso bata' is the Hindi-influenced name used in North Indian and restaurant contexts. Served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey source). Restaurant-level sodium may be higher than estimated; the 320 mg per 100 g figure reflects a moderate home-style preparation estimate.
